infobox.value: is the introduction of [fuel](/wiki/Fuel "Fuel") in an [internal combustion engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), most commonly [automotive engines](/wiki/Automotive_engine "Automotive engine"), by the means of a fuel injector. This article focuses on fuel injection in [reciprocating piston](/wiki/Reciprocating_engine "Reciprocating engine") and [Wankel rotary](/wiki/Wankel_engine "Wankel engine") engines.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Feature of internal combustion engines

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/PetrolDirectInjectionBMW.JPG/250px-PetrolDirectInjectionBMW.JPG)](/wiki/File:PetrolDirectInjectionBMW.JPG)

A cutaway model of a petrol direct-injected engine

**Fuel injection** is the introduction of [fuel](/wiki/Fuel "Fuel") in an [internal combustion engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), most commonly [automotive engines](/wiki/Automotive_engine "Automotive engine"), by the means of a fuel injector. This article focuses on fuel injection in [reciprocating piston](/wiki/Reciprocating_engine "Reciprocating engine") and [Wankel rotary](/wiki/Wankel_engine "Wankel engine") engines.

All compression-ignition engines (e.g., [diesel engines](/wiki/Diesel_engine "Diesel engine")), and many spark-ignition engines (i.e., [petrol (gasoline) engines](/wiki/Petrol_engines "Petrol engines"), such as [Otto](/wiki/Otto_engine "Otto engine") or [Wankel](/wiki/Wankel_engine "Wankel engine")), use fuel injection of one kind or another. Mass-produced diesel engines for passenger cars (such as the [Mercedes-Benz OM 138](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_OM_138 "Mercedes-Benz OM 138")) became available in the late 1930s and early 1940s, being the first fuel-injected engines for passenger car use.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In passenger car petrol engines, fuel injection was introduced in the early 1950s and gradually gained prevalence until it had largely replaced [carburettors](/wiki/Carburettor "Carburettor") by the early 1990s.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The primary difference between carburetion and fuel injection is that fuel injection [atomizes](/wiki/Atomizer_nozzle "Atomizer nozzle") the fuel through a small nozzle under high pressure, while carburetion relies on [suction](/wiki/Suction "Suction") created by intake air accelerated through a [Venturi tube](/wiki/Venturi_effect "Venturi effect") to draw fuel into the airstream.

The term *fuel injection* is vague and comprises various distinct systems with fundamentally different functional principles. The only thing all fuel injection systems have in common is the absence of carburetion.

There are two main functional principles of mixture formation systems for internal combustion engines: *internal* and *external*. A fuel injection system that uses external mixture formation is called a [manifold injection](/wiki/Manifold_injection "Manifold injection") system. There exist two types of manifold injection systems: *[multi-point](#Multi-point_injection)* (or *port*) and *[single-point](#Single-point_injection)* (or *[throttle body](/wiki/Throttle_body "Throttle body")*) injection.

Internal mixture formation systems can be separated into several different varieties of *direct* and *indirect* injection, the most common being the [*common-rail* injection](/wiki/Common_rail "Common rail"), a variety of direct injection. The term *electronic fuel injection* refers to any fuel injection system controlled by an [engine control unit](/wiki/Engine_control_unit "Engine control unit").

## System functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: System functions")]

The fundamental functions of a fuel injection system are described in the following sections. In some systems, a single component performs multiple functions.

### Pressurising fuel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pressurising fuel")]

Fuel injection is operated by spraying pressurised fuel into the engine. Therefore a device to pressurise the fuel is needed, such as a fuel pump.

### Metering fuel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Metering fuel")]

The system must determine the appropriate amount of fuel to be supplied and control the fuel flow to supply this amount.

Several early mechanical injection systems used relatively sophisticated helix-controlled injection pump(s) that both metered fuel and created injection pressure. Since the 1980s, [electronic systems](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics") have been used to control the metering of fuel. More recent systems use an electronic [engine control unit](/wiki/Engine_control_unit "Engine control unit") which meters the fuel and controls the [ignition timing](/wiki/Ignition_timing "Ignition timing") and various other engine functions.

### Injecting fuel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Injecting fuel")]

The fuel injector is effectively a [spray nozzle](/wiki/Spray_nozzle "Spray nozzle") that performs the final stage in the delivery of fuel into the engine. The injector is located in the [combustion chamber](/wiki/Combustion_chamber "Combustion chamber"), [inlet manifold](/wiki/Inlet_manifold "Inlet manifold") or – less commonly – the [throttle body](/wiki/Throttle#Throttle_body "Throttle").

Fuel injectors which also control the metering are called *injection valves*, while injectors that perform all three functions are called *[unit injectors](/wiki/Unit_injector "Unit injector")*.

## Direct injection systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Direct injection systems")]

Direct injection means that the fuel is injected into the main combustion chamber of each cylinder.[[3]](#cite_note-3) As air and fuel are mixed only inside the combustion chamber, air alone is sucked into the engine during the intake stroke. The injection scheme is always intermittent (either sequential or cylinder-individual).

Fuel is injected directly into the combustion chamber either with a blast of air[[4]](#cite_note-Teichmann-4) or hydraulically, with the former rendered obsolete in automotive engines in the early 20th century by the invention of the [precombustion chamber](/wiki/Precombustion_chamber "Precombustion chamber").

Typically, hydraulic direct injection systems spray fuel into the air inside the cylinder or combustion chamber. Direct injection can be achieved with a conventional helix-controlled injection pump, unit injectors, or a sophisticated common-rail injection system. The last is the most common system in modern automotive engines.

### Direct injection for petrol engines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Direct injection for petrol engines")]

Main article: [Petrol direct injection](/wiki/Petrol_direct_injection "Petrol direct injection")

During the 20th century, most petrol engines used either a [carburettor](/wiki/Carburettor "Carburettor") or indirect fuel injection. Use of direct injection in petrol engines has become increasingly common in the 21st century.

### Common-rail injection systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Common-rail injection systems")]

Main article: [Common-rail injection](/wiki/Common-rail_injection "Common-rail injection")

In a common-rail system, fuel from the fuel tank is supplied to a common header (called the [accumulator](/wiki/Hydraulic_accumulator "Hydraulic accumulator")), and then sent through tubing to the injectors, which inject it into the combustion chambers. The accumulator has a high-pressure relief valve to maintain pressure and return the excess fuel to the fuel tank. The fuel is sprayed with the help of a nozzle that is opened and closed with a [solenoid-operated](/wiki/Solenoid_valve "Solenoid valve") [needle valve](/wiki/Needle_valve "Needle valve").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Third-generation common-rail diesels use [piezoelectric](/wiki/Piezoelectric_motor "Piezoelectric motor") injectors for increased precision, with fuel pressures up to 300 [MPa](/wiki/Pascal_(unit) "Pascal (unit)") or 44,000 [psi](/wiki/Pound_per_square_inch "Pound per square inch").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The types of common-rail systems include *air-guided injection*[[7]](#cite_note-van_Basshuysen_62-7) and *spray-guided injection*.[[7]](#cite_note-van_Basshuysen_62-7)

### Unit injector systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Unit injector systems")]

Main article: [Unit injector](/wiki/Unit_injector "Unit injector")

Used by diesel engines, these systems include:

* Pumpe-Düse[[8]](#cite_note-Tschöke_295-8)
* Pump-rail-nozzle system[[8]](#cite_note-Tschöke_295-8)

### Helix-controlled pump systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Helix-controlled pump systems")]

This injection method was previously used in many diesel engines. Types of systems include:

* Lanova direct injection[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* Afterchamber injection[[10]](#cite_note-von_Fersen_131-10)
* G-System ([sphere](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere") combustion chamber)[[11]](#cite_note-Droscha_429-11)
* Gardner system (hemisphere combustion chamber)[[11]](#cite_note-Droscha_429-11)
* Saurer system ([torus](/wiki/Torus "Torus") combustion chamber)[[11]](#cite_note-Droscha_429-11)
* Flat piston (combustion chamber between piston and head)

### Air-blast injection systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Air-blast injection systems")]

Main article: [Air-blast injection](/wiki/Air-blast_injection "Air-blast injection")

### Other systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other systems")]

The [M-System](/wiki/M-System "M-System"), used in some diesel engines from the 1960s to the 1980s, sprayed the fuel onto the walls of the combustion chamber,[[12]](#cite_note-Droscha_433-12) as opposed to most other direct-injection systems which spray the fuel into the middle of the chamber.

## Indirect injection systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Indirect injection systems")]

### Manifold injection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Manifold injection")]

Main article: [Manifold injection](/wiki/Manifold_injection "Manifold injection")

Manifold injection systems are common in petrol-fuelled engines such as the [Otto engine](/wiki/Otto_engine "Otto engine") and the [Wankel engine](/wiki/Wankel_engine "Wankel engine"). In a manifold injection system, air and fuel are mixed outside the combustion chamber so that a mixture of air and fuel is sucked into the engine. The main types of manifold injections systems are *multi-point injection* and *single-point injection*.

These systems use either a *continuous injection* or an *intermittent injection* design.[[13]](#cite_note-Reif_107-13) In a continuous injection system, fuel flows at all times from the fuel injectors, but at a variable flow rate. The most common automotive continuous injection system is the
[multi-point](#Multi-point_injection) [Bosch K-Jetronic](/wiki/Jetronic#K "Jetronic") system, introduced in 1974 and used until the mid-1990s by various car manufacturers. Intermittent injection systems can be *sequential*, in which injection is timed to coincide with each cylinder's intake stroke; *batched*, in which fuel is injected to the cylinders in groups, without precise synchronization to any particular cylinder's intake stroke; *simultaneous*, in which fuel is injected at the same time to all the cylinders; or *cylinder-individual*, in which the engine control unit can adjust the injection for each cylinder individually.[[13]](#cite_note-Reif_107-13)

#### Multi-point injection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Multi-point injection")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Marvel_Fuel_Injection_System_Cross_Section.png/250px-Marvel_Fuel_Injection_System_Cross_Section.png)](/wiki/File:Marvel_Fuel_Injection_System_Cross_Section.png)

Schematic of a mechanical port injection system

[Multi-point injection](/wiki/Manifold_injection#Multi-point_injection "Manifold injection") (also called 'port injection') injects fuel into the intake ports just upstream of each cylinder's [intake valve](/wiki/Intake_valve "Intake valve"), rather than at a central point within an intake manifold.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Typically, multi-point injected systems use multiple fuel injectors,[[15]](#cite_note-Lohner_64-15) but some systems, such as GM's central port injection system, use tubes with poppet valves fed by a central injector instead of multiple injectors.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

#### Single-point injection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Single-point injection")]

[Single-point injection](/wiki/Manifold_injection#Single-point_injection "Manifold injection") (also called 'throttle-body injection')[[17]](#cite_note-17) uses one injector in a [throttle body](/wiki/Throttle_body "Throttle body") mounted similarly to a [carburettor](/wiki/Carburettor "Carburettor") on an [intake manifold](/wiki/Intake_manifold "Intake manifold"). As in a carburetted induction system, the fuel is mixed with the air before entering the intake manifold.[[15]](#cite_note-Lohner_64-15) Single-point injection was a relatively low-cost way for automakers to reduce [exhaust emissions](/wiki/Vehicle_emissions_control "Vehicle emissions control") to comply with tightening regulations while providing better "driveability" (easy starting, smooth running, no engine stuttering) than could be obtained with a carburettor. Many of the carburettor's supporting components—such as the air filter, intake manifold, and fuel line routing—could be used with few or no changes. This postponed the redesign and tooling costs of these components. Single-point injection was used extensively on American-made passenger cars and light trucks during 1980–1995, and in some European cars in the early and mid-1990s.
In the US, the G10 engine in the 2000 Chevrolet Metro became the last engine available on an American-sold vehicle to use throttle body injection.

### Diesel engines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Diesel engines")]

In indirect-injected diesel engines (as well as Akroyd engines), there are two combustion chambers: the main combustion chamber, and a [pre-chamber](/wiki/Precombustion_chamber "Precombustion chamber") (also called an ante-chamber)[[18]](#cite_note-18) that is connected to the main one. The fuel is injected only into the pre-chamber (where it begins to combust), and not directly into the main combustion chamber. Therefore, this principle is called *indirect injection*. There exist several slightly different indirect injection systems that have similar characteristics.[[19]](#cite_note-von_Fersen_273-19)

Types of indirect injection used by diesel engines include:

* [Precombustion-chamber injection](/wiki/Indirect_injection#Precombustion_chamber "Indirect injection")[[19]](#cite_note-von_Fersen_273-19)
* [Air-cell chamber injection](/wiki/Indirect_injection#Air_cell_chamber "Indirect injection")[[20]](#cite_note-von_Fersen_130-20)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Hot-bulb injection")]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: History")]

### 1870s–1930s: early systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 1870s–1930s: early systems")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Lufteinblasflaschen.jpg/250px-Lufteinblasflaschen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lufteinblasflaschen.jpg)

Air-blast injection system for an 1898 diesel engine

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Moteur_de_l_Antoinette_VII_Musee_du_Bourget_P1010360.JPG/250px-Moteur_de_l_Antoinette_VII_Musee_du_Bourget_P1010360.JPG)](/wiki/File:Moteur_de_l_Antoinette_VII_Musee_du_Bourget_P1010360.JPG)

Mechanical port injection system on a 1906 [Antoinette 8V](/wiki/Antoinette_8V "Antoinette 8V") engine

In 1872, [George Bailey Brayton](/wiki/George_Brayton "George Brayton") obtained a patent on an internal combustion engine that used a pneumatic fuel injection system, also invented by Brayton: [air-blast injection](/wiki/Air-blast_injection "Air-blast injection").[[21]](#cite_note-21): 413 In 1894, [Rudolf Diesel](/wiki/Rudolf_Diesel "Rudolf Diesel") copied Brayton's air-blast injection system for the diesel engine, but also improved it.[[22]](#cite_note-22): 414 He increased the air blast pressure from 390–490 kPa (57–71 psi) to 6,400 kPa (920 psi).[[23]](#cite_note-23): 415 In the meantime, the first manifold injection system was designed by Johannes Spiel in 1884, while working at *Hallesche Maschinenfabrik* in Germany.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 1891, the British [Herbert-Akroyd oil engine](/wiki/Herbert_Akroyd_Stuart#Hornsby-Akroyd_engine "Herbert Akroyd Stuart") became the first engine to use a pressurised fuel injection system.[[25]](#cite_note-rwallis-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) This design, called a [hot-bulb engine](/wiki/Hot-bulb_engine "Hot-bulb engine") used a 'jerk pump' to dispense [fuel oil](/wiki/Fuel_oil "Fuel oil") at high pressure to an injector. Another development in early diesel engines was the pre-combustion chamber, which was invented in 1919 by Prosper l'Orange[[27]](#cite_note-Mau_1984_p._11-27) to avoid the drawbacks of air-blast injection systems. The pre-combustion chamber made it feasible to produce engines in size suitable for automobiles and [MAN Truck & Bus](/wiki/MAN_Truck_%26_Bus "MAN Truck & Bus") presented the first direct-injected diesel engine for trucks in 1924.[[20]](#cite_note-von_Fersen_130-20) Higher pressure diesel injection pumps were introduced by [Bosch](/wiki/Robert_Bosch_GmbH "Robert Bosch GmbH") in 1927.

In 1898, German company [Deutz AG](/wiki/Deutz_AG "Deutz AG") started producing four-stroke petrol [stationary engines](/wiki/Stationary_engine "Stationary engine")[[28]](#cite_note-Cummins_1971-28) with manifold injection.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The 1906 [Antoinette 8V](/wiki/Antoinette_8V "Antoinette 8V") aircraft engine (the world's first V8 engine) was another early four-stroke engine that used manifold injection. The first petrol engine with direct-injection was a two-stroke aircraft engine designed by Otto Mader in 1916.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Another early spark-ignition engine to use direct-injection was the 1925 [Hesselman engine](/wiki/Hesselman_engine "Hesselman engine"), designed by Swedish engineer Jonas Hesselman.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) This engine could run on a variety of fuels (such as oil, kerosene, petrol or diesel oil)[[32]](#cite_note-32) and used a [stratified charge](/wiki/Stratified_charge_engine "Stratified charge engine") principle whereby fuel is injected towards the end of the compression stroke, then ignited with a [spark plug](/wiki/Spark_plug "Spark plug").

The [Cummins](/wiki/Cummins "Cummins") *Model H* diesel truck engine was introduced in America in 1933.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In 1936, the [Mercedes-Benz OM 138](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_OM_138 "Mercedes-Benz OM 138") diesel engine (using a precombustion chamber) became one of the first fuel-injected engines used in a mass-production passenger car.[[34]](#cite_note-von_Fersen_274-34)

### 1940s–1950s: WWII aircraft and early direct-injection petrol engines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 1940s–1950s: WWII aircraft and early direct-injection petrol engines")]

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), several petrol engines for aircraft used direct-injection systems, such as the European [Junkers Jumo 210](/wiki/Junkers_Jumo_210 "Junkers Jumo 210"), [Daimler-Benz DB 601](/wiki/Daimler-Benz_DB_601 "Daimler-Benz DB 601"), [BMW 801](/wiki/BMW_801 "BMW 801"), and the [Shvetsov ASh-82FN (M-82FN)](/wiki/Shvetsov_ASh-82 "Shvetsov ASh-82"). The German direct-injection systems were based on diesel injection systems used by Bosch, Deckel, Junkers and l'Orange.[[35]](#cite_note-35) By around 1943, the [Rolls-Royce Merlin](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_Merlin "Rolls-Royce Merlin") and [Wright R-3350](/wiki/Wright_R-3350 "Wright R-3350") had switched from traditional carburettors to fuel-injection (called "pressure carburettors" at the time), however these engines used throttle body [manifold injection](#Manifold_injection), rather than the direct-injection systems of the German engines. From 1940, the [Mitsubishi Kinsei 60 series](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Kinsei "Mitsubishi Kinsei") engine used a direct-injection system, along with the related [Mitsubishi Kasei](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Kasei "Mitsubishi Kasei") engine from 1941. In 1943, a low-pressure fuel injection system was added to the [Nakajima Homare Model 23](/wiki/Nakajima_Homare "Nakajima Homare") radial engine.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The first mass-produced petrol direct-injection system was developed by Bosch and was initially used in small automotive two-stroke petrol engines. Introduced in the 1950 [Goliath GP700](/wiki/Goliath_GP700 "Goliath GP700") small saloon, it was also added to the [Gutbrod Superior](/wiki/Gutbrod_Superior "Gutbrod Superior") engine in 1952. This mechanically controlled system was essentially a specially lubricated high-pressure diesel direct-injection pump of the type that is governed by the vacuum behind an intake throttle valve.[[37]](#cite_note-37) A Bosch mechanical direct-injection system was also used in the straight-eight used in the 1954 [Mercedes-Benz W196](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_W196 "Mercedes-Benz W196") Formula One racing car. The first four-stroke direct-injection petrol engine for a passenger car was released the following year, in the [Mercedes-Benz 300SL](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_300SL "Mercedes-Benz 300SL") sports car.[[38]](#cite_note-38) However the engine suffered lubrication problems due to petrol diluting the engine oil,[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) and subsequent Mercedes-Benz engines were switched to a manifold injection design. Likewise, most petrol injection systems prior to the 2000s used the less-expensive manifold injection design.

### 1950s–1970s: manifold injection for petrol engines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 1950s–1970s: manifold injection for petrol engines")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/1959_Chevrolet_Corvette_C1_V8_283_cui_Fuel_Injection.jpg/250px-1959_Chevrolet_Corvette_C1_V8_283_cui_Fuel_Injection.jpg)](/wiki/File:1959_Chevrolet_Corvette_C1_V8_283_cui_Fuel_Injection.jpg)

1950s [Rochester Ramjet](/wiki/Rochester_Ramjet "Rochester Ramjet") mechanical port injection system (on a [Chevrolet 283 engine](/wiki/Chevrolet_small-block_engine_(first_and_second_generation)#283 "Chevrolet small-block engine (first and second generation)"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/BMW_Engine_M88_from_a_M1.JPG/250px-BMW_Engine_M88_from_a_M1.JPG)](/wiki/File:BMW_Engine_M88_from_a_M1.JPG)

1970s [Kugelfischer](/wiki/Kugelfischer "Kugelfischer") mechanical port injection system (on a [BMW M88](/wiki/BMW_M88 "BMW M88") engine)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/K-Jet_MT1.jpg/250px-K-Jet_MT1.jpg)](/wiki/File:K-Jet_MT1.jpg)

Components of a 1970s [K-Jetronic](/wiki/K-Jetronic "K-Jetronic") electronic port injection system

Throughout the 1950s, several manufacturers introduced their manifold injection systems for petrol engines. [Lucas Industries](/wiki/Lucas_Industries "Lucas Industries") had begun developing a fuel injection system in 1941, and by 1956 it was used in Jaguar racing cars.[[41]](#cite_note-41) At the [1957 24 Hours of Le Mans](/wiki/1957_24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "1957 24 Hours of Le Mans"), the 1st to 4th placed cars were [Jaguar D-Type](/wiki/Jaguar_D-Type "Jaguar D-Type") entries using a Lucas fuel injection system.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Also in 1957, General Motors introduced the [Rochester Ramjet](/wiki/Rochester_Ramjet "Rochester Ramjet") option, consisting of a fuel injection system for the V8 engine in the Chevrolet Corvette. During the 1960s, fuel injection systems were also produced by [Hilborn](/wiki/Stuart_Hilborn "Stuart Hilborn"),[[43]](#cite_note-howgood-43) [SPICA](/wiki/SPICA "SPICA")[[44]](#cite_note-44) and [Kugelfischer](/wiki/Kugelfischer "Kugelfischer").

Up until this time, the fuel injection systems had used a mechanical control system. In 1957, the American [Bendix Electrojector](/wiki/Bendix_Electrojector "Bendix Electrojector") system was introduced, which used analogue electronics for the control system. The Electrojector was intended to be available for the [Rambler Rebel](/wiki/Rambler_Rebel "Rambler Rebel") mid-size car, however reliability problems meant that the fuel injection option was not offered.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-auto.howstuffworks.com-49) In 1958, the [Chrysler 300D](/wiki/Chrysler_300_letter_series#1958_300D "Chrysler 300 letter series"), [DeSoto Adventurer](/wiki/DeSoto_Adventurer "DeSoto Adventurer"), [Dodge D-500](/wiki/1958_Dodge#D-500 "1958 Dodge") and [Plymouth Fury](/wiki/Plymouth_Fury "Plymouth Fury") offered the Electrojector system, becoming the first cars known to use an electronic fuel injection (EFI) system.[[50]](#cite_note-1958DeSoto-50)

The Electrojector patents were subsequently sold to Bosch, who developed the Electrojector into the Bosch [D-Jetronic](/wiki/D-Jetronic "D-Jetronic").[[51]](#cite_note-51) The D-Jetronic was produced from 1967-1976 and first used on the [VW 1600TL/E](/wiki/Volkswagen_Type_3 "Volkswagen Type 3"). The system was a speed/density system, using engine speed and intake manifold air density to calculate the amount of fuel to be injected. In 1974, Bosch introduced the [K-Jetronic](/wiki/K-Jetronic "K-Jetronic") system, which used a continuous flow of fuel from the injectors (rather than the pulsed flow of the D-Jetronic system). K-Jetronic was a mechanical injection system, using a plunger actuated by the intake manifold pressure which then controlled the fuel flow to the injectors.[[52]](#cite_note-von_Fersen_256-52)

Also in 1974, Bosch introduced the [L-Jetronic](/wiki/L-Jetronic "L-Jetronic") system, a pulsed flow system which used an [air flow meter](/wiki/Mass_flow_sensor#Moving_vane_meter "Mass flow sensor") to calculate the amount of fuel required. L-Jetronic was widely adopted on European cars during the 1970s and 1980s. As a system that uses electronically controlled fuel injectors which open and close to control the amount of fuel entering the engine, the L-Jetronic system uses the same basic principles as modern electronic fuel injection (EFI) systems.

### 1980s–present: digital electronics and common-rail injection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fuel_injection&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 1980s–present: digital electronics and common-rail injection")]

Prior to 1979, the electronics in fuel injection systems used [analogue electronics](/wiki/Analogue_electronics "Analogue electronics") for the control system. The [Bosch Motronic](/wiki/Motronic "Motronic") multi-point fuel injection system (also amongst the first systems where the [ignition system](/wiki/Ignition_system "Ignition system") is controlled by the same device as the fuel injection system) was the first mass-produced system to use [digital electronics](/wiki/Digital_electronics "Digital electronics"). The [Ford EEC-III](/wiki/Ford_EEC#EEC-III "Ford EEC") single-point fuel injection system, introduced in 1980, was another early digital fuel injection system.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-von_Fersen_262-54) These and other electronic manifold injection systems (using either [port injection](#Multi-point_injection) or [throttle-body injection](#Single-point_injection)) became more widespread through the 1980s, and by the early 1990s they had replaced carburettors in most new petrol-engined cars sold in developed countries.

The aforementioned injection systems for petrol passenger car engines – except for the 1954–1959 *Mercedes-Benz 300 SL* – all used [manifold injection](#Manifold_injection) (i.e. the injectors located at the intake ports or throttle body, instead of inside the combustion chamber). This began to change when the first mass-produced [petrol direct injection](/wiki/Petrol_direct_injection "Petrol direct injection") system for passenger cars was a [common rail](/wiki/Common_rail "Common rail") system introduced in the 1997 [Mitsubishi 6G74](/wiki/Mitsubishi_6G7_engine#6G74 "Mitsubishi 6G7 engine") V6 engine.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) The first common-rail system for a passenger car diesel engine was the [Fiat Multijet](/wiki/Fiat_JTD_engine#1.9 "Fiat JTD engine")[*[broken anchor](/wiki/MOS:BROKENSECTIONLINKS "MOS:BROKENSECTIONLINKS")*] straight-four engine,[[57]](#cite_note-57) introduced in the 1999 *Alfa Romeo 156 1.9 JTD* model. Since the 2010s, many petrol engines have switched to direct-injection (sometimes in combination with separate manifold injectors for each cylinder). Similarly, many modern diesel engines use a common-rail design.

[Stratified charge](/wiki/Stratified_charge "Stratified charge") injection was used in several petrol engines in the early 2000s, such as the [Volkswagen 1.4 FSI engine](/wiki/List_of_discontinued_Volkswagen_Group_petrol_engines#1.4_R4:_FSI_63-77kW "List of discontinued Volkswagen Group petrol engines") introduced in 2000. However, the stratified charge systems were largely no longer in use by the late 2010s, due to increased exhaust emissions of NO*x* gasses and particulates, along with the increased cost and complexity of the systems.
